examples.title: Stop-and-Wait ARQ Sender Implementation
examples.description: Pseudocode for sender side of Stop-and-Wait ARQ protocol showing frame transmission and acknowledgment handling.
examples.code:
PROCEDURE SendFrame(frame_number)
 TRANSMIT(frame_number, data)
 START_TIMER(frame_number)
 WHILE NOT ACK_received
 IF TIMEOUT
 RETRANSMIT(frame_number)
 RESET_TIMER
 END IF
 WAIT_FOR_ACK
 END WHILE
 STOP_TIMER
 CANCEL_ACK
 RETURN SUCCESS

PROCEDURE ReceiveACK(ack_number)
 IF ack_number == expected_ack
 expected_frame = expected_frame + 1
 RETURN TRUE
 ELSE
 RETURN FALSE
 END IF
examples.explanation: The sender transmits one frame and starts a timer. It waits for acknowledgment before sending the next frame. If timeout occurs (no ACK received within expected time), the frame is retransmitted. The loop continues until successful acknowledgment is received.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of frames
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) - only stores current frame number and timer state

examples.title: Sliding Window Sender Implementation
examples.description: Pseudocode showing how sender manages a sliding window of frames for efficient transmission.
examples.code:
CONSTANT WINDOW_SIZE = 5
VARIABLE send_base = 0
VARIABLE next_seq_num = 0
VARIABLE packet_buffer = []

PROCEDURE SendData(data)
 IF next_seq_num < send_base + WINDOW_SIZE
 packet_buffer[next_seq_num] = data
 TRANSMIT(next_seq_num, data)
 IF send_base == next_seq_num
 START_TIMER(send_base)
 END IF
 next_seq_num = next_seq_num + 1
 ELSE
 WAIT_FOR_WINDOW_ADVANCE
 END IF

PROCEDURE HandleACK(ack_num)
 IF ack_num > send_base
 send_base = ack_num
 IF send_base == next_seq_num
 STOP_TIMER
 ELSE
 RESET_TIMER(send_base)
 END IF
 END IF
examples.explanation: The sender maintains two pointers: send_base (oldest unacked frame) and next_seq_num (next frame to send). The window slides forward as ACKs are received. Multiple frames can be in flight, improving utilization. Timer is managed for the oldest unacknowledged frame.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) per frame transmission, O(n) for ACK processing
examples.spaceComplexity: O(WINDOW_SIZE) - stores frames in current window

examples.title: TCP Flow Control Receiver Implementation
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating receiver-side TCP flow control with window advertisement.
examples.code:
VARIABLE recv_buffer[MAX_WINDOW]
VARIABLE read_pointer = 0
VARIABLE write_pointer = 0
VARIABLE expected_seq = 0

PROCEDURE ReceivePacket(seq_num, data, length)
 available_space = MAX_WINDOW - write_pointer
 
 IF seq_num == expected_seq
 STORE_IN_BUFFER(seq_num, data)
 expected_seq = expected_seq + length
 WRITE_POINTER = expected_seq MOD MAX_WINDOW
 
 WHILE data_available_in_buffer
 DELIVER_TO_APPLICATION
 READ_POINTER = (read_pointer + 1) MOD MAX_WINDOW
 END WHILE
 ELSE IF seq_num > expected_seq
 STORE_IN_BUFFER(seq_num, data) // Out-of-order
 END IF
 
 advertised_window = (MAX_WINDOW - write_pointer + read_pointer) MOD MAX_WINDOW
 SEND_ACK(expected_seq, advertised_window)

PROCEDURE GetAdvertisedWindow
 RETURN (MAX_WINDOW - write_pointer + read_pointer) MOD MAX_WINDOW
examples.explanation: The receiver maintains a circular buffer with read and write pointers. It advertises the available buffer space (advertised window) in every ACK. When the window reaches zero, the sender must wait. Out-of-order frames may be buffered for later delivery.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for packet processing, O(n) for in-order delivery to application
